How Microgrids Can Boost Economic Development for a Community and Its Residents
===============================================================================

When we talk about a community microgrid, we typically mean a larger scale project that benefits an entire community, not just a [single customer](https://www.ameresco.com/portfolio-item/london-district-catholic-school-board/) or building. A community microgrid could be owned and operated by the community itself, by an integrator like Ameresco, or even by an innovative and forward-thinking utility.

Much has been written about the benefits that renewable microgrids bring to communities in terms of resiliency and opportunities to reduce carbon emissions, and while generating clean, reliable energy is a great reason to invest in a community microgrid, it’s not the only one that should be considered. Microgrids also offer broader economic benefits that can position a community for long-term growth and economic development.

![Image 2: Slemon Park Microgrid DJI_0073](https://www.ameresco.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/03/Slemon-Park-Microgrid-DJI_0073-495x400.jpg)

### **Microgrids create new jobs**

As the North American energy infrastructure moves toward a more microgrid-driven architecture over the next 10-15 years, microgrid manufacturing, installation and operations will accelerate, which will require skilled, local workers.

Workers will be needed to build wind, solar or other distributed energy resources for large-scale community microgrid projects. After the microgrid is commissioned, experts in control and operations management must be employed. It’s true that the artificial intelligence (AI) used in microgrid control systems can handle a lot of the operations management, but microgrid systems still require on-the-ground experts to handle what AI cannot.

At Ameresco, our mantra has always been to hire locally where we can; sometimes training and developing a local workforce is even part of the scope of work. As microgrid adoption increases, we expect to see waves of new, good paying, renewable energy jobs. Additionally, there will be tremendous opportunities for the regional universities, colleges and vocational schools that are training or retraining the local workforce to fill those openings.

From training to construction, operations and maintenance, a community microgrid can provide a significant number of jobs and improve the economics of the entire area.

### **Microgrid resiliency attracts new companies to the area**

In addition to clean energy jobs, a community microgrid is the perfect opportunity to partner with the local utility to strengthen a community’s energy infrastructure. While some utilities see microgrids as a threat to their business model, others see them as a new tool that can provide reliable, greener electricity to their customers.

Regions with reliable electrical quality — a key benefit of microgrids — are better able to attract new tech and manufacturing businesses. That means more jobs, higher tax revenues for the local government and more opportunities for the community to grow.

For example, the [Slemon Park microgrid](https://www.ameresco.com/portfolio-item/slemon-park-microgrid-pei/) on Canada’s Prince Edward Island (PEI) is designed to help the community create a thriving commercial industrial hub. Developed in partnership with PEI Energy, the microgrid has a 10-MW solar system and can island from the larger PEI grid in case of an outage.

For a renewables-based microgrid to provide resiliency, you must have an energy storage system in play. Slemon Park’s solar and battery energy storage systems are co-located on-site and share the same utility interconnect. Additionally, individual battery energy storage units are deployed to each leaseholder’s business. The combination of these two energy storage methods provides the long-term, reliable electrical quality that makes the area attractive to businesses looking to relocate.

### **Renewable microgrids attract businesses looking to meet their ESG goals**

When a community microgrid includes renewable energy sources, it allows a municipality to make an impact on its environmental commitments in ESG goals through the reduction of its greenhouse gas emissions. It’s also a great selling feature for like-minded businesses.

This is what’s happening on PEI, which is focused on becoming the first Canadian province to go net zero, and the Slemon Park microgrid will help the island achieve that goal by generating reliable, zero emissions electricity.

The microgrid is also attracting companies looking for a way to meet their own climate goals. Relocating to a region that’s on the path to net zero is an easy way to reduce carbon emissions.

### **Microgrids and decarbonization**

Microgrids provide controllable, resilient, and green energy within a local area, and they will be a big part of the infrastructure we need to decarbonize our economy. Further, microgrid proliferation is expected to provide more jobs and a tremendous economic boost to communities across the country.

### The Rise of Clean Energy Microgrids

This free informational resource details why clean energy microgrids make sense for healthcare, higher education, government, businesses and more.All microgrids offer greater electric reliability. But the clean energy microgrid does more. It also reduces carbon emissions and helps organizations reach sustainability goals – an achievement increasingly valued by the healthcare community, business customers, government constituents, and college students, parents and donors.

![Logic20/20](https://logic2020.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/06/logo-new-1.png)

# Microgrid Costs and Benefits: Transforming Energy Resilience for Utilities

by [Alec Kostovny](https://logic2020.com/author/alec-kostovny/ "Posts by Alec Kostovny") | Feb 25, 2025

![](https://logic2020.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/microgrids-transforming-energy-resilience-and-cost-efficiency.jpg "microgrids-transforming-energy-resilience-and-cost-efficiency")

11-minute read

*Quick summary: How [microgrids](/industries/energy-utilities/microgrids-resilient-energy-solutions/ "Microgrids for Resilience and Sustainability") are enhancing energy resilience, reducing costs, and transforming grid operations for utilities through innovative, real-world applications.*

As the energy landscape evolves, [microgrids](https://logic2020.com/insight/optimizing-green-hydrogen-how-microgrids-enhance-energy-resilience/ "Optimizing green hydrogen: How microgrids enhance resilience") represent a transformative approach to power generation and distribution, offering unprecedented flexibility and resilience. These localized electrical networks operate independently or in conjunction with the main grid, enabling utilities to enhance reliability, reduce costs, and integrate renewable energy sources more effectively. While microgrids entail initial capital costs for upgrades and improvements, they offer long-term operational savings and reduced electricity expenses, making them a cost-effective solution. 

In this article, we explore the evolving role of microgrids in modern energy systems, highlighting key use cases—from critical infrastructure to remote communities—and examining the economic and operational factors driving increased adoption. We will also discuss the challenges utilities face in implementing microgrid solutions and the technological innovations shaping their future.

## What is a microgrid?

A microgrid is a localized electrical network that can operate independently or in conjunction with the main power grid and is capable of generating, distributing, and controlling the flow of electricity within a defined area. Unlike traditional grid systems, microgrids can disconnect from the broader network and function autonomously during emergencies or peak demand periods, utilizing local generation sources like small generators, wind turbines, and rooftop solar panels.

Providing operators and utilities flexibility and resilience with respect to cost and power reliability, microgrids have increased in prevalence due to [a burgeoning DER market](https://logic2020.com/insight/how-the-role-of-der-aggregators-is-changing-and-how-ai-can-help-them-adapt/ "AI’s role in DER aggregators’ evolution"). Microgrids grew in popularity in the early 2000s as an experimental approach to localized energy management, initially developed only as backup power sources. Today, microgrids have evolved into sophisticated energy solutions deployed across critical infrastructure, military bases, remote communities, and urban centers. They represent a pivotal technology for enhancing grid resilience, integrating renewable energy, and providing localized energy independence.

Energy storage components, such as Battery Energy Storage Systems (BESS) and dispatchable loads, play a crucial role in enhancing energy reliability and flexibility within microgrids. These components interact with renewable energy sources and demand response programs to optimize energy use and support grid services during peak demand periods.

Microgrids are not necessarily new to the utility landscape; however, approaches to determining optimal use cases and evaluating where and when a microgrid makes sense have changed in recent years. Historically, microgrids have been viewed as a unique (and expensive) solution with applications mostly focusing on addressing facilities with extremely high reliability needs like hospitals or military bases. But the cost analyses are changing. Given increased spending on distribution, microgrids are starting to proliferate in different areas where the dual benefit of reliability and cost savings from storage and renewable generation are strong.

## Understanding Microgrid Costs

Microgrid costs can vary widely depending on the size and configuration of the system. The cost of a microgrid can be broken down into several components, each playing a crucial role in the overall functionality and efficiency of the system. 

**Distributed Energy Resources (DERs)**: These are the backbone of any microgrid, providing the primary sources of power. DERs can include solar panels, wind turbines, and other renewable energy sources. The cost of solar panels, for instance, can range from $2 to $5 per watt, depending on the technology and installation specifics. Wind turbines and other renewable sources have similar cost structures, influenced by factors such as location, scale, and technology. 

**Energy Storage Components**: Energy storage is vital for stabilizing the grid and ensuring a reliable power supply during outages. Batteries, a common form of energy storage, can range from $200 to $500 per kilowatt-hour, depending on the type and quality. Different battery chemistries and designs offer a balance of performance and cost. Popular lithium-ion batteries have had cost reductions in recent years due to manufacturing momentum worldwide, but other newer technologies like sodium-ion and flow batteries may support different price points for microgrid developers. 

**Microgrid Controller**: The microgrid controller is the brain of the system, managing the flow of energy between the DERs, energy storage, and the grid. This component can range from $50,000 to $200,000, depending on the complexity and capabilities required. A sophisticated controller can optimize energy use, reduce costs, and enhance the overall efficiency of the microgrid. 

By understanding these cost components, stakeholders can better plan and budget for microgrid projects, ensuring they achieve the desired balance of reliability, efficiency, and cost-effectiveness. 

Other things that affect cost of microgrid: 

Implementation – What is the current infrastructure at the desired site? Do lots of new electrical components need to be installed or do simple hookups achieve the network that is needed?  

Grid connection – What is the current signed deal with the utility? PPA or rate-based? Is the cost of electricity in the area high or low relative to the project’s needs? 

Cost-benefit – Even if all costs of MG components are low, does the project actually need this backup power? Or are they fine with reliability of local grid?

## Components of a Microgrid System

A microgrid system is a complex network of various components working together to provide reliable and efficient power. Understanding these components is key to appreciating how microgrids function and their benefits. 

**Distributed Energy Resources (DERs)**: DERs are the primary power sources within a microgrid. They include solar panels, wind turbines, fuel cells, and other renewable energy technologies. These resources generate electricity locally, reducing dependence on the main grid and enhancing energy security. 

**Energy Storage**: Energy storage components, such as batteries, play a critical role in microgrid systems. They store excess energy generated by DERs and release it when needed, such as during peak demand periods or power outages. This capability not only stabilizes the grid but also provides backup power, ensuring continuous energy supply. 

**Microgrid Controller**: The microgrid controller is essential for managing the flow of energy within the system. It coordinates the operation of DERs and energy storage, optimizing energy use and reducing costs. The controller can also facilitate interactions with the larger electricity grid, enabling the microgrid to sell excess power back to the grid or draw power when needed. 

**Grid Connection**: While microgrids can operate independently, a connection to the main electricity grid provides additional flexibility and revenue opportunities. Through net metering and other programs, microgrids can sell excess energy back to the grid, creating additional revenue streams and enhancing the overall economic viability of the system. 

These components work in harmony to create a resilient, efficient, and flexible energy system, capable of meeting the diverse needs of modern energy consumers. 

## Key use cases for microgrids

Utilities are continuously looking for strategic solutions to address complex challenges in grid management and energy reliability, and microgrids currently appear to address these needs in some areas to address. The dollar-per-minute loss of power to critical infrastructure—where the value of backup generation is calculated for cost analyses—can vary drastically depending on the asset. [In locations where this value is extremely high](https://logic2020.com/insight/microgrids-vs-derms-data-center-energy-strategy/ "How microgrids and DERMS are reshaping energy strategies for data centers"), like hospitals or key infrastructure, microgrids have made sense due to their high reliability scores and ability to disconnect from large grids where an outage might take longer to fix.

The costs of microgrid projects can vary significantly based on size, configuration, and additional factors like location and technology used. 

Companies assess resilience and the cost implications of microgrid projects, considering both the initial capital costs and potential operational savings. They face challenges due to rising electricity costs and the reliability of grid power, making microgrids an attractive solution for economic benefits and enhanced resilience.

![](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20viewBox='0%200%20975%20482'%3E%3C/svg%3E "microgrid-diagram")
![](https://logic2020.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/microgrid-diagram.png "microgrid-diagram")

Specialized backup power solutions for key assets have provided a key value for microgrids, albeit not one that has widespread potential. The custom aspect of microgrid design provides unprecedented flexibility, but the network requires expensive engineering and design that have limited its growth to a select few areas for most of the last few decades.

### Use case #1: Emergency backup

Within a key infrastructure that would incur huge financial losses in case of a power outage, microgrids have been installed to reduce exposure to larger grid outages. Organizations with emergency backup power needs that extend beyond single load-generation co-locating (such as backup generators) have turned to microgrids to power critical assets.

As previously mentioned, hospitals or military bases that have above average reliability concerns needed microgrids to ensure safe and constant power that trumped some of the cost concerns of recent decades. Emergency backup uses constitute by far the biggest use case for microgrids today, as the high implementation costs can be offset by the necessity for extremely reliable power. Additionally, integrating hydrogen fuel cell technology within microgrid systems provides a cost-effective and efficient solution for emergency backup power, complementing renewable sources like solar PV and battery energy storage systems. 

### Use case #2: Community utility

Other common uses have occurred in communities like neighborhoods or universities that desired control over their power supply. These may be rural areas where power supply needs were not being met in a desirable way by the regional utility, or simply an area where it made sense to utilize a distributed energy resource (DER) rather than buying power. Microgrid solutions can address customer needs, reduce costs, and improve resilience for communities. Electric vehicles can be integrated with microgrids to enhance building energy management, providing flexibility and resilience in energy use. Universities like NYU were some of the first areas to create microgrids as a backup power source for their community. The school’s network demonstrated its utility during Hurricane Sandy, as the microgrid system was able to restore power much more quickly than the main grid for the rest of the city. The showcase of power reliability in microgrid-connected neighborhoods in the aftermath of storms is a big reason why microgrid prevalence has started to increase.

### Use case #3: Islands

Remote communities have become critical pioneers in microgrid development, primarily due to their unique geographic and economic circumstances that make traditional power infrastructure impractical. These isolated communities have emerged as pioneering adopters in the microgrid space, serving as vital proof-of-concept implementations. The substantial expenses typically associated with extending traditional power infrastructure across vast distances or challenging terrain have made microgrid installations appear far more financially attractive in these locations compared to other potential applications in more accessible areas.

Microgrids can also help reduce demand charges for remote communities by managing energy use more efficiently, particularly during peak demand periods. This not only lowers network charges but also enhances the overall cost-effectiveness of energy systems in these areas. 

These island implementations have provided an invaluable testing ground where current microgrid technologies are being refined and overall system costs are gradually being reduced through practical learning, operational experience, and optimization. The ongoing dialogue surrounding power solutions for geographically isolated communities offers particularly valuable insights into current microgrid technology capabilities and limitations.

These remote locations face amplified versions of common energy challenges, forcing them to weigh different DER options more carefully than less vulnerable regions. With a calculus slightly different from that of most potential applications, remote regions and island communities have shown to be the first growing group of microgrid installations.

**NYU’s microgrid demonstrated its utility during Hurricane Sandy, as the microgrid system was able to restore power much more quickly than the main grid for the rest of the city.**

## Key challenges and considerations for microgrid implementation

The deployment of microgrids represents a transformative opportunity in the energy sector, yet organizations face an array of obstacles spanning regulatory inconsistencies, substantial capital requirements, and technical integration challenges. Testing and validating microgrid operations, particularly during power outages or stress tests, is crucial to ensure their ability to maintain critical operations and provide energy resilience. 

The expected outcomes of microgrid systems include significant financial savings and extended component lifespans. For instance, the expected life of certain components is around 10 years, and projected savings often exceed initial expected values due to varying electricity costs and operational strategies. 

### Utility regulation

Inconsistent, outdated regulatory frameworks create significant barriers to widespread microgrid adoption. Utility regulations, frequently designed around centralized power generation models, do not accommodate decentralized energy architectures. These policies can slow the process of implementing a microgrid solution that needs utility approval for islanding strategies and bidding into energy markets.

Performance standardization is equally crucial, given the need to develop comprehensive metrics and validation processes that can ensure consistent reliability, efficiency, and safety across different microgrid implementations.

### High cost of integration

The extent of the upfront capital required represents another critical hurdle, as the initial cost of microgrid infrastructure can be prohibitively expensive for many organizations, requiring innovative financing mechanisms and clear ROI strategies. The lack of standardized designs presents engineering challenges that increase complexity and project timelines, crippling some projects before they even start.

Renewable energy sources play a crucial role in electricity generation within microgrid systems, enhancing energy security and sustainability. Diverse equipment, communication protocols, and legacy systems must be seamlessly integrated to create microgrid networks. The availability of more plug-and-play solutions would provide an avenue for cost reductions and represent a large step forward for developing cheaper solutions that can benefit utilities and consumers alike.

Cybersecurity also emerges as an area of concern, with increasingly complex and interconnected microgrid systems presenting potential vulnerabilities that could compromise critical infrastructure. The applications where reliable microgrids can penetrate most easily are also the sectors where security is most necessary. Increased communication between energy generation infrastructure like controllers, inverters, and meters means the risk of compromised systems is significant in the planning and development of these projects.

### Grid defection and utility revenue losses

The changing distribution network and economics surrounding increased penetration of DERs offers another nuanced look at microgrids and how they will affect the energy grid. Increasing a consumer’s likelihood of grid-defect through rising electricity costs will cause pricing issues that microgrids may be able to address—or may, in fact, make worse. The value of removing a load center from the grid is changing as the economics of DERs and microgrids change, and this development will provide opportunities to microgrid developers, but also harm to utilities. Projected savings and energy use related to microgrids indicate significant cost reductions for facilities, which can evolve over time with changing electricity rates and technological advancements. 

The balance between technological advancement and smart implementation practices will continue to play out as microgrids can become more cost effective and valuable as utility and community assets.

## Emerging applications in energy operations

Microgrid deployments are slowly moving beyond their initial conceptualization as backup power systems towards becoming dynamic, intelligent energy management platforms. Today’s emerging market sees microgrids as active, adaptive energy ecosystems that can dynamically balance multiple energy sources, integrate sophisticated renewable technologies, and provide real-time grid optimization.

A microgrid controller plays a crucial role in managing peak energy demand and ensuring recovery during outages. Advanced controllers can optimize operations and reduce costs, although implementing these systems in microgrid projects can be challenging and costly. 

As emerging applications for microgrids point to different revenue streams and cost-saving options, the numbers are slowly starting to look more attractive. Given recent innovations and operational experience in design and supply chain management, utilities are increasingly looking at options to implement these grid solutions. A few select applications are emerging as promising areas.

### Decentralized energy production models

[DERs derive their primary value from enhancing flexibility at the grid edge](https://logic2020.com/insight/harnessing-der-orchestration-for-a-more-flexible-resilient-grid/ "Insight: DER orchestration: post-publication tasks"), where power distribution meets end users. While DERs represent utilities’ initial step toward decentralized power generation, microgrids extend this concept further by delivering more substantial cost savings and reliability improvements.

The National Renewable Energy Laboratory (NREL) has been at the forefront of innovations in renewable microgrid technology. NREL’s research has led to simplified controls for microgrids that can operate efficiently during power outages, emphasizing the effectiveness of using renewable energy sources with minimal setup, which could significantly enhance disaster recovery efforts. 

Microgrids offer enhanced visibility and control over power generation assets compared to today’s popular behind-the-meter DERs because they are concentrated in a defined area instead of being scattered across a distribution network. This consolidated control allows operators to monitor and manage flexible generation capacity more effectively and have further control of decentralized production in the regional area.

Microgrids can now also offer load balancing schemes to local areas with more capability than most DERs. The grid-tie of the microgrid is key in this flexibility, offering the ability to dynamically control power flow and island (disconnect from the grid) if needed. Islanding of a microgrid offers the refinement of load balancing models and offers a utility further flexibility.

The result of implementing more microgrids is a more sophisticated approach to power management that builds on the foundational benefits of individual DERs while offering more system-wide advantages.

### Flexible cost-saving schemes

New microgrids have evolved to offer two compelling benefits: enhanced reliability and flexible cost savings. This dual advantage is becoming increasingly important as renewable energy markets face new challenges with locational pricing, where an abundance of renewable generation can create pricing inefficiencies in certain regions.

Key considerations in achieving cost savings with microgrids include the integration of renewable energy technologies, transitioning from gas-using systems to electrification, and ensuring the overall resilience and reliability of building energy systems. These considerations are crucial in adapting to the complexities associated with energy demand changes driven by climate change and technological advancements. 

Microgrids address these challenges by providing flexibility in both power generation and pricing optimization within specific geographical areas. They can help manage periods of excess low-cost power by [strategically storing energy and adjusting local generation patterns](https://logic2020.com/insight/microgrid-control-strategy-philosophy/). For example, a microgrid can store energy when prices are low and deploy it during peak demand periods, providing value to both its immediate users and the Regional Operator.

Unlike a utility-scale storage solution, a microgrid can offer the dual purpose of reliability for its co-located load and storage solutions for the larger grid. When a microgrid installation can also provide crucial storage capacity and peak generation capabilities to the regional grid, its financial value proposition becomes substantially stronger. This enhanced value makes microgrid investments more attractive to stakeholders, as the combined benefits of reliability and grid services can justify the initial capital expenditure.

As these economic advantages become more apparent, the accelerated adoption of microgrid technology across different regions and applications is much more possible.

**When a microgrid installation can provide crucial storage capacity and peak generation capabilities to the regional grid, its financial value proposition becomes substantially stronger.**

### Geographic and sector-specific opportunities

Microgrid deployment has evolved into a sophisticated solution that adapts to diverse geographical and institutional contexts, each with its unique energy challenges. With unique energy solutions needed for many different geographic and sector areas, microgrids have an adaptability that can fit multiple needs.

**Industrial applications** demonstrate perhaps the most dramatic transformation in microgrid utilization. Contemporary industrial microgrids prioritize net-zero carbon emission strategies while generating economic benefits through energy arbitrage. Their granular energy management capabilities typically reduce operational costs alongside their emission reduction strategies, making them increasingly attractive to business operators focused on both sustainability and cost efficiency.

**Rural and agricultural communities** have witnessed their own microgrid emergence. Basic electrification solutions have now evolved into comprehensive rural development platforms. These microgrid systems now support agricultural productivity through reliable power delivery and local energy entrepreneurship, creating new economic opportunities. Critical loads for agricultural producers can now be met with microgrid technologies, enabling further reliability and the potential for cost savings in a critical area for farming businesses.

In the **healthcare** sector, microgrids have transformed from simple backup power systems into sophisticated resilience platforms. These advanced systems offer predictive maintenance capabilities and ensure not just power continuity, but optimal power quality for critical medical equipment and operations.

The versatility of microgrid technology continues to expand as systems become more sophisticated and interconnected. Each sector—whether urban, industrial, rural, or healthcare—presents unique opportunities for implementation, showcasing the technology’s remarkable adaptability. This evolution represents a fundamental shift in energy infrastructure, moving away from traditional one-size-fits-all grid approaches toward tailored solutions that address specific local needs while contributing to broader grid stability and sustainability goals.

## The future of microgrids: Emerging trends and innovations

The future of microgrids lies at the intersection of technological innovation, artificial intelligence, and sophisticated energy management strategies. As the traditional view of microgrids as one-dimensional backup power systems melts away, the future points to a rapid advancement of even more intelligent features that will complement the digitization of the energy industry.

However, it is important to consider the additional costs associated with microgrid projects, especially when evaluating the levelized cost of electricity (LCOE). While microgrids with distributed generation (DG) offer significant benefits, the probability of surviving prolonged outages incurs additional costs, which significantly raises the LCOE as the duration of the outages increases. 

The future of microgrids is intrinsically linked to emerging technological innovations that promise to reshape energy infrastructure. Artificial intelligence and machine learning are poised to transform microgrids into predictive, self-optimizing systems capable of real-time energy management, with advanced algorithms enabling unprecedented levels of efficiency and responsiveness.

In urban environments, modern microgrids leverage advanced AI and machine learning to manage complex energy demands with unprecedented precision. These systems seamlessly integrate distributed energy resources including rooftop solar, battery storage, and electric vehicle charging infrastructure, while creating economic value through active participation in energy markets via demand response and energy trading.

Energy storage technologies, particularly next-generation battery systems and hydrogen storage, will likely allow microgrids to store and manage renewable energy with increasing sophistication. Different battery types like solid state, sodium ion, and flow batteries (to name but a few) offer varied storage solutions that can target specific applications for cheaper and more efficient systems. The convergence of these technologies suggests that microgrids will evolve from localized power solutions to intelligent, adaptive energy ecosystems that can dynamically balance generation, storage, and consumption across multiple interconnected networks.

## Looking ahead to the future of microgrids

The trajectory of microgrid development suggests a future where energy systems are no longer centralized but instead become adaptive, localized, and inherently more responsive to community and environmental needs. Backup power generation and remote locations offer current windows into how microgrids can offer reliable power to consumers, albeit at a high cost. As custom solutions, they are inherently held back specialized engineering needs which have historically limited their adoption due to high implementation costs.

Advanced controls play a crucial role in microgrid systems, streamlining operations and improving the functionality of renewable energy technologies, which is essential for ensuring stability and reliability in decentralized power systems

However, by democratizing energy generation, enhancing grid reliability, and providing pathways for renewable energy integration, microgrids are positioned to play a transformative role in creating more resilient and technologically sophisticated energy ecosystems. More applications with dual benefits like reliability and cost savings can lead to more attractive ROI opportunities and in turn create learning opportunities for others to improve costs even more. These are the sorts of questions utilities will begin to raise in modernizing their power delivery schemes, and they will need help doing it.

# Microgrid Benefits: Eight Ways a Microgrid will Improve your Operation … and the World

![petovarga / Shutterstock.com](https://img.microgridknowledge.com/files/base/ebm/microgridknowledge/image/2023/02/petovarga_shutterstock_1965445276.63da7f0798c0c.png?auto=format,compress&fit=fill&fill=blur&q=45&w=250&width=250 "petovarga / Shutterstock.com")

**Eight microgrid benefits**

***1. A microgrid improves electric reliability***

Among [microgrid](https://www.microgridknowledge.com/about-microgrids/article/11429017/what-is-a-microgrid) benefits, electric reliability has gotten the most attention since 2012 when Superstorm Sandy knocked out power to about 8[million](http://www.cnn.com/2013/07/13/world/americas/hurricane-sandy-fast-facts/index.html) electric customers in 15 states and the District of Columbia. Some outages lasted two weeks or more. People suffering in the darkness noticed that neighboring buildings still had their lights on. Those were facilities with microgrids.

Power outages aren’t only an inconvenience, they can be dangerous. When a Manhattan hospital’s backup generators failed during the storm, medical personnel had to evacuate ill babies by carrying them down long, dark flights of stairs as they manually pumped air into their lungs.

Microgrids keep the power flowing by disconnecting — or islanding — from the central grid when it begins to fail. The microgrid’s generators, and possibly batteries, then serve the microgrid’s customers until power is restored on the central grid.

***2. A microgrid enhances resilience/recovery***

Closely related to electric reliability is the idea of energy resilience. While reliability is about keeping the power on, resilience describes the ability to avoid power outages in the first place or to recover quickly if they do occur.

Resilience is also among the microgrid benefits that came to the fore following Superstorm Sandy. Its importance has been underscored in a series of lesser storms since.

In some cases, a microgrid immediately restores power to an entire building or operation, leaving occupants barely aware a disturbance occurred.

In other cases, a microgrid is programmed to restore only critical services within a facility. So a college campus, for example, might configure its microgrid to restore power to research labs and dining halls, but perhaps not swimming pools or peripheral academic offices. With critical operations up and running, the university can maintain a minimal and crucial level of service. Once the central grid is repaired, the facility can resume normal operations more quickly because it did not have to shut down completely.

What is a [microgrid](https://microgridknowledge.com/microgrid-defined/)?

Utilities are now investigating installing microgrids for their central headquarters so that they can restore power faster to their customers during a massive outage. The utilities would coordinate workers and information from these electrified command centers.

***3. A microgrid can lower energy costs for consumers and businesses***

Microgrids can both reduce costs and provide a revenue stream for their customers. They reduce costs through the efficient management of energy supply. They supply revenue by selling energy and services back to the grid. This gives consumers a new kind of control in energy markets. They no longer just consume energy but also can produce and control it through their microgrids. Such customers are called prosumers.

Microgrids can earn revenue by providing ancillary services to the central grid. Ancillary services provide support functions for the grid, such as frequency control and spinning reserve.

Advanced microgrids also are adept at leveraging energy pricing. Electricity prices fluctuate throughout the day based on expected and historical demand. An advanced microgrid controller, known as the brain of the system, can leverage this fluctuation on its customers’ behalf. The microgrid does this by orchestrating the play of its assets with the rise and fall of electric pricing on the grid. When demand for grid energy is high and grid prices increase, the controller may signal the microgrid to use more of its own resources to avert paying the higher prices. If the microgrid has excess capacity, it may sell it back to the grid.

Because it uses its assets to maximize value over time, the microgrid serves its customers with both short- and long-term pricing planning. The extent to which it can do this, however, will depend on the sophistication of the microgrid controller and regional wholesale market rules.

Microgrids also can gain economic benefit by joining utility demand response programs or by participating in state and federal clean energy programs, such as state renewable portfolio standard initiatives or federal production tax credits. Some states have grant programs specifically for microgrids.

In areas where electricity costs are high — such as the Northeast and California — microgrids may be able to consistently provide energy at a lower cost. Microgrid customers then receive benefits, such as reliability and cleaner energy, yet pay lower prices for energy.

***4. A microgrid improves the environment and promotes clean energy***

Many businesses and communities establish clean energy goals to conserve energy and reduce the environmental impact of their power generation.

Microgrids can employ a wide range of green power production technologies. These include solar, wind, fuel cells, combined heat and power (CHP) plants, and energy storage technologies. Natural gas generators, used in many CHP plants, fall on the cleaner side of fossil fuels.

Microgrids integrate these renewables into the energy mix intelligently. They seamlessly balance the variable output of renewable energy with traditional generation assets. In doing so, the microgrid overcomes the downside of solar and wind energy, which is that they only generate power when the wind blows or sun shines. With no human intervention, a microgrid can tap into other resources when renewable energy is unavailable.

Advanced microgrids can also be programmed to achieve specific sustainability goals, such as use of the lowest carbon resources, to the maximum extent possible.

***5. A microgrid strengthens the central grid***

In addition to serving its own customers, a microgrid benefits neighbors as well when it’s used to strengthen the broader electric grid.

It does this in a few ways. One is by augmenting normal grid operation; for example, by participating in demand response programs or providing ancillary services.

Microgrids also can help ease strain on the central grid during periods of peak demand. They act as an additional resource grid operators can call upon during these periods.

The use of microgrids also averts line loss — the dissipation of electricity as it travels over wires. Microgrids are built close to the customers they serve, unlike large central power plants that may have to push their electricity hundreds of miles to reach their customers. Less line loss means we use more of the electricity we produce. In doing so, we avert the need to build more power plants and transmission lines to serve the central grid.

In fact, because microgrids can be less expensive to build and maintain than new grid substations, transmission lines or other grid infrastructure, they can act as “non-wires alternatives” — a resource built to improve reliability in lieu of more expensive grid repairs or upgrades.

***6. A microgrid bolsters cybersecurity***

A massive ransomware attack in May 2017 heightened worldwide concern about cybersecurity. Affecting 150 nations, the malware infected hundreds of thousands of businesses and institutions from British hospitals to FedEx in the US.

The US power grid has not been inflicted with a cyberattack that causes loss of power, but many experts are concerned about its vulnerability and are taking proactive measures. Installing microgrids is among them.

Military installations and utilities have taken keen notice of cybersecurity. For example, Ameren Illinois has developed a 1.475-MW project that is one of the most technologically advanced utility-scale microgrids in North America. Ameren is the first utility to install a [military-grade cybersecure microgrid controller.](https://microgridknowledge.com/cybersecure-microgrid/) In addition to advanced controls, the microgrid includes wind, solar, natural gas and energy storage.

The distributed architecture of a microgrid makes it more resistant to cyberattack. Should one generator be attacked, the microgrid has other power sources to rely on.

***7. A microgrid brings economic value to society***

Microgrids offer economic value to society in several ways. First, they avert loss of product and workdays during a power outage. Second, they attract high quality employers to a region. Third, as local energy plants, they keep jobs within the community.

By islanding from the grid during a power outage — and continuing to supply customers via on-site generators — microgrids avert significant economic loss. Businesses do not have to close; workers can get to their jobs.

Power outages are costly, especially to research facilities, data centers, manufacturers and grocery stores that lose product or services.

The cost of outages to the US economy ranges from $25 billion to $70 billion annually, depending on the number of storms, according to a Congressional Research Service study. Just a 30-minute interruption could cost a medium or large business or factory more than $15,000.

Given how expensive outages are to business, it is no surprise that enterprises dependent on premium power are attracted to communities with microgrids. By offering reliable power, these communities can attract high quality employers, such as data centers and pharmaceutical manufacturers.

It’s also important to note that the construction and operation of the microgrid creates local jobs. The community is not using power from a plant hundreds of miles away. As local energy, a microgrid benefits the local economy directly.

For all of these reasons, some communities now make microgrids part of their economic planning.

***8. A microgrid improves community well-being***

When a storm or other disaster knocks out power, at best it’s an inconvenience; at worst it’s a health and safety threat, especially if critical facilities cannot operate. As a result, more and more communities are installing microgrids to serve hospitals, police stations, fire departments, communications centers and wastewater treatment plants.

The geographic region served by the microgrid becomes an island of power. Sometimes shelters, grocery stores and gas stations are included within the microgrid’s service area. The neighborhood becomes a place of refuge, where community members can come to buy food, get clean water, charge cell phones and gas up cars.

Some communities provide microgrids for public housing where residents are restricted in their ability to evacuate during an emergency. Others protect the power supply to direct care providers such as homeless shelters, nursing homes and medical satellite facilities.

Here are just three examples of how a microgrid benefits society:

**Conclusion**

A microgrid benefits its customers and society in many ways. It keeps the lights on when the central grid fails. It offers a way to upgrade the distribution grid, harden infrastructure and protect vulnerable communities. Through sophisticated, automated energy management, a microgrid can bolster clean energy use and can create economic value for customers — as well as the broader grid. And, finally, using a microgrid promotes local control, or what some call the democratization of energy and the rise of the prosumer.

# How microgrids are powering greener, more resilient real estate

Energy management

Sustainability services

Infrastructure and renewables

Energy

Authors

Rhian Nicholson

Convert to PDF

As cities and industries ramp up their electrification efforts, aging energy infrastructure is buckling under the growing demand.

Power disruptions and price volatility have been exacerbated by utility expansion backlogs and increasingly frequent extreme weather events, fuelling an unprecedented market for microgrids.

These decentralized energy systems integrate renewable energy sources and energy storage to supplement central utilities, enhancing grid stability and helping buildings maintain normal operations during outages.

Traditionally used to provide resilient power where local grids are unreliable, often in remote areas, microgrids are rapidly building a presence in more urban settings.

“Power demands are increasing faster than utilities can build infrastructure, and climate change is impacting the reliability of power,” says Douglas Mackenzie, Vice President, Energy Resilience at JLL. “In today’s environment, microgrids are gaining traction among owners and occupiers looking to mitigate long-term risk, meet sustainability targets, and drive profitability.”

## Microgrids across industries

Military facilities have long used microgrids for energy security, with the U.S. Army aiming to install a microgrid in [**all its bases by 2035**](https://www.microgridknowledge.com/editors-choice/article/11427449/army-to-equip-all-bases-with-microgrids-by-2035-as-part-of-carbonfree-electricity-goal). Now, microgrids are being adopted across industries such as healthcare and data centres where power outages create critical situations or have a steep business cost.

“When the power goes out, our clients lose money and the welfare of our communities is threatened,” says Mackenzie. “Microgrids are one of the best tools we have to ensure the continuity of our businesses in a more volatile climate and future-proof our infrastructure for the electrification of our economy.”

For example, airports, which face [**millions of dollars in lost revenue**](https://www.reuters.com/article/atlanta-airport-delta-air/atlanta-airport-power-outage-cost-delta-air-lines-up-to-50-million-idINKBN1EE2HQ) when power disruptions bring operations to a halt, are now recognizing the value of microgrids. Take New York City’s JFK airport, where a planned microgrid on the New Terminal One will be the [**largest rooftop solar array in the city**](https://www.prnewswire.com/news-releases/alphastruxure-to-design-construct-and-operate-jfks-new-terminal-one-microgrid-creating-the-largest-rooftop-terminal-solar-array-in-the-us-301731016.html). It aims to minimize the impact of power disruptions by generating clean onsite energy that will keep the terminal functioning through grid outages and stabilize utility costs.

Falling implementation costs will continue to drive demand across other industries, with the highest forecasted growth in the commercial and industrial sector. In the U.S., the Inflation Reduction Act offers tax credits and grants for microgrid installations, spurring growth across industries.

Local incentive and grant programs are also driving growth. For example, the California Energy Commission’s Advanced Solar Emergency Microgrids Grant partially funded the [**installation of microgrids at eight municipal facilities**](https://www.sandiego.gov/sustainability-mobility/energy/muni-energy/me-programs-projects/microgrids) in San Diego to provide energy resilience for the city’s emergency response services and support the city’s longer-term goal to run on 100% renewable power by 2035.

![Multiple container partking infront of building](https://s7d1.scene7.com/is/image/joneslanglasalle/jll-how-microgrids-are-powering-greener-more-resilient-real-estate-desktop-1127x388?qlt=85&ts=1739958029262&dpr=off)

## Driving wider sustainability initiatives

It’s not just real estate owners who are installing microgrids to better manage their own or their tenants’ energy needs. As traditional utilities providers grapple with modernizing their infrastructure, microgrids can help manage peak demand on central grids and deliver uninterrupted power to customers, deferring costly and time-intensive network upgrades.

“In addition to providing local carbon free energy, microgrids optimize electricity supply and demand to support electrification investments,” says Mackenzie. “We’re seeing a compelling value proposition for microgrids as a tool to enable electric vehicle fleet charging.”

In Montgomery County, Maryland, after extreme weather cut out local power for days, municipal leaders established a [**smart, microgrid-enabled bus depot**](https://alphastruxure.com/case-studies/montgomery_county_brookville_bus_depot/) that supported the electrification of its diesel fleet while also providing backup power for the community.

These resiliency and sustainability benefits are supporting the investment case for microgrids, even when local grids offer lower electricity rates, notes Mackenzie.

Their modular infrastructure also means microgrids can be scaled and updated with new advancements in renewable energy, battery storage and energy management, helping to futureproof today’s installations.

## Keeping the lights on

From community resilience hubs to mid-sized hospital to university campuses to large manufacturing and industrial zones, microgrids are poised to become a common feature of tomorrow’s built environment.

“The model for power and energy infrastructure is undergoing a transformation to a decentralized state where consumers play a more active role in securing sustainable, cost effective, and resilient energy,” says Mackenzie.

Down the line, microgrids will integrate clean fuels such as green hydrogen, longer-duration energy storage and other new energy technologies that will help cement the transition underway.

“The beauty of a microgrid is that it’s shaped to the unique needs of a community and is inherently scalable,” says Mackenzie. “By incorporating various sources of energy, microgrids will continue to evolve with the most advanced carbon-free technologies.”

“As corporations make bold sustainability commitments and climate change impacts the bottom line, there is a growing need to invest in energy infrastructure that supports corporate goals and the operational needs of our businesses. Microgrids are key to building a carbon free and resilient future.”

What is a microgrid?
--------------------

The DOE defines the microgrid as ‘‘a group of interconnected loads and distributed energy resources within clearly defined electrical boundaries that acts as a single controllable entity with respect to the grid. A microgrid can connect and disconnect from the grid to enable it to operate in both grid-connected or island mode.’’

For more information from DOE, please review their [Microgrids Overview](https://www.energy.gov/sites/default/files/2024-01/2024-01-18%20Microgrid%20Overview%20Fact%20Sheet.pdf), which includes cost estimates, design considerations, and best practice tips.

![Image 3: Visual depiction of a how a microgrid, using power supply and load centers, integrates into the utility grid.](https://dlg.colorado.gov/sites/dlg/files/How-Microgrids-Use-Power-Supply.png)

Credit: NORESCO

Why are microgrids beneficial?
------------------------------

Microgrids provide efficient, low-cost, clean energy, enhance local resiliency, and improve reliability of the regional electric grid.

### Benefits:

*   Resilience
    *   A microgrid provides customers with energy resilience by avoiding power outages in the first place, or quickly recovering if they do occur. In the case of an outage, the microgrid can be programmed to restore power to an entire facility, or just the most critical components. Once grid power is restored, the facility can resume normal operations more quickly because it did not have to shut down completely.
    *   The Microgrid for Community Resilience grant program focuses on enhancing resilience for community anchor institutions (such as schools, fire stations, and hospitals). In rural communities, those centralized community spaces are critical - especially in times of extreme weather or emergencies.

*   Reliability
    *   Microgrids keep the power flowing during an outage by disconnecting from the grid in “island” mode. The system’s controller switches from grid power to the microgrid’s local power sources when it senses an outage. Solar, back-up generators, battery energy storage or the microgrid’s other distributed energy sources then serve its customers until the grid’s power is restored.
    *   A microgrid can be used to strengthen the broader electric grid by augmenting normal grid operations or easing the strain on the central grid during periods of peak demand.

*   Cost savings
    *   Microgrids can reduce the energy costs of their customers by efficiently managing energy supply, which helps customers budget for energy costs in both the short and long term.

*   Promoting clean energy
    *   Microgrids can use a wide range of clean energy generation technology (solar, wind, fuel cells, combined heat and power plants, energy storage). Using these diverse energy sources together ensures that the microgrid can overcome any downsides of a specific technology.

Examples of Microgrids as Community Resilience:
-----------------------------------------------

### National:

[New York University](https://microgrid-symposiums.org/microgrid-examples-and-demonstrations/new-york-university-microgrid/): During Superstorm Sandy, NYU’s microgrid successfully islanded from the local distribution grid and continued to provide reliable power to much of the NYU campus, also serving as a resilience hub for the community. Resilience hubs are community-serving facilities that support residents, coordinate communication, distribute resources, and reduce carbon pollution while enhancing quality of life.

### Colorado:

*   [Red Feather Lakes Microgrid (Larimer County)](https://pvrea.coop/the-co-op/news/microgrid-brings-increased-resiliency-red-feather-lakes/)​: Community-based microgrid and resilience hub serving ​library, post office, local businesses, and critical services ​ such as fire response, EMS, and telecommunications in a rural, mountainous region
*   [Northside Aztlan Community Center (Fort Collins)](https://microgridnews.com/fort-collins-deploys-aztlan-community-center-resilience-microgrid/): Community center designed to provide backup power during the fire season to enable the center to host emergency operations and as a resilience hub. Partially funded by DOLA.
*   [San Miguel Power Association Microgrid](https://crea.coop/2022/04/21/smpa-helps-county-obtain-microgrid-grant/): Microgrid system containing two solar PV and energy storage microgrid systems. The system connected two crucial parts of the San Miguel County sheriff’s department: the annex building in Norwood and the Ilium sheriff office near Telluride.This project centered around mission-critical loads. Partially funded by DOLA.
*   [Pueblo Community Health Center - East Side Center](https://www.nachc.org/the-first-zero-net-energy-health-center-in-the-u-s-is-here/) [in development]: This location will be the first net-zero energy health center site in the United States (as of 2020). Health centers considering solar microgrids and back-up battery systems may want to check out the [Community Health Access to Resilient Green Energy (CHARGE) partnership](https://chargepartnership.org/). Charge is a collaboration between NACHC, Collective Energy, and Capital Link and provides energy options for health centers supporting communities that are vulnerable to grid outages and have patient populations disproportionately affected chronic disease, poverty and racial and ethnic health inequities.

To learn more about microgrids, visit [Microgrid Knowledge](https://www.microgridknowledge.com/) or [Think Microgrid](https://www.thinkmicrogrid.org/) websites.

Microgrid classification and types
----------------------------------

### Level 1 “Single Building Microgrid”

This classification of microgrid includes a single distributed energy resource (DER) system such as a solar photovoltaic (PV), combined heat and power (CHP), or fuel cell system that serves a single building interconnected to the grid at a single utility meter location.

*   MCR Funded Project: San Miguel Power Association - Ridgway
    *   Resilience Hub supporting a public community room
    *   4.8 kW solar and 37 kW battery, 1.5 days of backup power 

### Level 2 “Partial Feeder” or “Campus Microgrid”

This classification of microgrid includes either a single or multiple DER system serving multiple buildings, interconnected to the grid at a single utility meter location. Level 2 microgrids can island whole campuses or partial feeders from the distribution grid. A feeder is an electrical line from a utility substation, serving customers within a defined local distribution area. Examples of this classification are a campus setting such as a college or university, healthcare/hospital campus, pharmaceutical complex, or military base.

*   MCR Funded Project: Poudre Valley Rural Electric Association - Livermore
    *   Battery installed at the Livermore Fire Station and serving a retail store, restaurant, school, post office, gas station & more
    *   70 kW Solar (State funding) and 350 kW battery (Federal funding)

### Level 3 “Full Feeder” or “Community Microgrid”

This classification of microgrid includes a single or multiple DER system serving multiple buildings or customers, however the loads and generation sources may not be interconnected at the same utility meter location. Instead, these advanced microgrids will have one point of common coupling (PCC), where the microgrid can be operated independently from the utility grid. The individual buildings or customers may be independently connected to the larger distribution grid and through the microgrid PCC and some customers may even have Level 1 or Level 2 microgrids that support the operation of the community microgrid.

*   **MCR Funded Project: Platte River Power Authority - Estes Park**
    *   Leasing a 5 MW (20 MWh) battery on a feeder line in Estes Park, CO
    *   Serves critical facilities such as Protection District and Hospital

**For more examples on various classifications, please read our CRO case study**[**Energy Resilience: Three Levels of Microgrids Across Larimer County**](https://coresiliency.colorado.gov/energy-resilience-three-levels-of-microgrids-across-larimer)**.**
